narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 50.7% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use for rejection to protect the program's outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your overall profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 34, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 3, upper-middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among the key elements that shaped this decision, your LSAT score stood out as the strongest positive signal, as it suggested a solid potential that nudged the model toward expecting you to pass, performing better than it might have otherwise. However, your Year 1 decile ranking of 3 was the most influential drag, far below the average of 6 for those who passed the bar, signaling to the model early academic struggles that strongly pushed toward a failure prediction. Your family income in the upper-middle range also contributed somewhat negatively, since while it's common among passers (about 45% share that bracket), it didn't offset the other concerns in your specific case as powerfully as it does for others. Compared to typical admitted students—who often have LSATs around 37, undergraduate GPAs near 3.24, and higher early deciles—your metrics placed you below the passing averages in several critical areas, tipping the balance despite the full-time commitment that aligns with 93% of successful bar takers. The model weighed these top influences—LSAT favorably, but Year 1 decile and income unfavorably—as the primary drivers, overriding smaller positives. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven call to prioritize bar passage rates for the school's reputation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that key strength, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering evidence of academic improvement since Year 3 to shift those decile perceptions. Many students in similar positions reapply successfully after targeted improvements, so use this as motivation to build an even stronger case next time. With focused effort, you can align your profile more closely with those who pass and get admitted.
